title: Git Internals
content:
## 🧠 Git Architecture: Beyond Commands, Understanding the System

Most beginners think Git is just a backup tool. In reality, Git is a **Content-Addressable File System** — a sophisticated database that stores every version of every file your project has ever had. Understanding how it works under the hood makes you a dramatically more effective developer.

> **Git ≠ GitHub.** Git is the local version control tool installed on your machine. GitHub is a cloud platform (website) for hosting Git repositories online.

---

## 1️⃣ How Git Stores Data: The Three Object Types

Git stores data as three types of objects, each identified by a **SHA-1 hash** (a unique 40-character fingerprint):

### Blob (Binary Large Object)
A blob stores the **raw content of a single file** — without a filename or any metadata. Two files with identical content share one blob.

### Tree
A tree is like a **directory listing** — it maps filenames to blobs, and can also point to other trees (sub-directories). A tree represents the structure of a directory at a point in time.

### Commit
A commit points to a **top-level tree** (the project root) and contains metadata:
- Author name and email
- Timestamp
- Commit message
- Pointer to the **parent commit(s)**

This chain of commits is your project's full history.

---

## 2️⃣ The Three Stages of Git

```
┌────────────────┐    git add    ┌─────────────┐    git commit    ┌─────────────────┐
│ Working Dir    │ ──────────→   │ Staging Area│ ───────────────→ │ Repository (.git)│
│ (your files)   │               │ (Index)     │                  │ (committed hist) │
└────────────────┘               └─────────────┘                  └─────────────────┘
```

- **Working Directory**: Your actual files on disk as you edit them.
- **Staging Area (Index)**: Files you've marked for inclusion in the NEXT commit. This is the "prep zone".
- **Repository (`.git` folder)**: The database where all your committed history lives.

---

## 3️⃣ The `.git` Directory

When you run `git init`, Git creates a hidden `.git` folder. **This IS your repository.** Delete it and you lose all history. Key contents:

```
.git/
  objects/    ← All blobs, trees, and commit objects
  refs/       ← Branch and tag pointers
  HEAD        ← Points to the current branch
  config      ← Repository configuration
  COMMIT_EDITMSG ← Last commit message
```

---

## 4️⃣ Core Commands Explained

```bash
git init              # Create a new .git directory (start tracking)
git status            # Show state of working dir + staging area
git add index.html    # Stage a specific file
git add .             # Stage all changes
git commit -m "msg"   # Create a snapshot with a message
git log               # Show commit history (hash, author, message)
git log --oneline     # Compact, one-line-per-commit view
```

---

## 5️⃣ HEAD — Where You Are

`HEAD` is a special pointer that shows **which branch and commit you're currently on**. It's like a "You Are Here" marker on a map.

```bash
cat .git/HEAD
# ref: refs/heads/main
```

When you make a commit, `HEAD` moves forward to the new commit. When you switch branches, `HEAD` moves to that branch's tip.

---

## 6️⃣ Commits as Snapshots, Not Diffs

Git does NOT store diffs (what changed). Each commit stores a **complete snapshot** of the project. For efficiency, unchanged files point to the same blob objects — so snapshots don't duplicate data.

---

## 7️⃣ Tags

A **tag** is a named pointer to a specific commit. Unlike branches (which move with new commits), tags are permanent:

```bash
git tag v1.0          # Lightweight tag on current commit
git tag -a v1.0 -m "First release"  # Annotated tag with message
git tag               # List all tags
```

Tags are used to mark release versions.

---

## 8️⃣ The `.gitignore` File

A `.gitignore` file tells Git to **never track certain files**. You must commit the `.gitignore` itself.

```bash
# .gitignore
node_modules/    # Never commit dependencies
.env             # Never commit secrets!
.DS_Store        # macOS system files
dist/            # Built files
*.log            # Any file ending in .log
```

- `*` is a wildcard that matches any characters.
- `!` negates a rule (un-ignores something).
- Lines starting with `#` are comments.

---

## 9️⃣ Undoing Changes

```bash
git restore index.html          # Discard unstaged changes in a file
git restore --staged index.html # Unstage a file (remove from staging)
git revert abc123               # Create a new commit that undoes a previous commit
git reset --soft HEAD~1         # Undo last commit but keep changes staged
git reset --hard HEAD~1         # ⚠️ DANGER: Undo last commit AND discard changes
```

---

## 💡 Summary

| Concept | Description |
|---|---|
| **Blob** | File content, identified by SHA-1 hash |
| **Tree** | Directory structure mapping names to blobs |
| **Commit** | Snapshot + metadata + parent pointer |
| **HEAD** | Pointer to current branch/commit |
| **Staging Area** | Prep zone before committing |
| `.git/` | The repository database |
| **Tag** | Permanent label for a commit |
| `.gitignore` | Files Git never tracks |
| `*` wildcard | Matches any number of characters in gitignore |

questions.question_text: What does Git use to represent the contents of a single file?
questions.explanation: A Blob stores the content of a file, but not its name or metadata.

questions.question_text: Where does Git store all its internal data and history in a project?
questions.explanation: The .git directory contains the database, configuration, and history of your repo.

questions.question_text: Which area contains the files you have modified but not yet committed?
questions.explanation: The Staging Area is the middle ground where you prepare content for a commit.

questions.question_text: What command initializes a new Git repository?
questions.explanation: git init creates the hidden .git folder.

questions.question_text: Which command adds files to the Staging Area?
questions.explanation: git add marks changes to be included in the next snapshot.

options: A backup of the entire computer
A snapshot of your project at a specific point in time
questions.explanation: Commits are the building blocks of Git history.

questions.question_text: What command shows the current state of your working directory?
questions.explanation: git status tells you which files are modified, staged, or untracked.

questions.question_text: How do you view the history of commits in your repo?
questions.explanation: git log displays the commit ID, author, and message for every commit.

options: A pointer to the current branch/commit you are working on
The first line of a file
questions.explanation: HEAD moves whenever you switch branches or make a new commit.

questions.question_text: Which command undoes changes in your working directory to match the last commit?
questions.explanation: Checkout discards local changes (modern Git also uses 'git restore').

questions.question_text: What is a 'Blob' in Git internals?
questions.explanation: Git stores file contents as blobs, decoupled from filenames.

questions.question_text: What is a 'Tree' object in Git?
options: A representation of a directory and its contents
questions.explanation: Trees map filenames to blobs and other nested trees.

questions.question_text: Which command allows you to name a specific commit (e.g., 'v1.0')?
questions.explanation: Tags are permanent labels for specific points in history.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the .gitignore file?
options: To list files that Git should never track (e.g., passwords, node_modules)
To hide files from the user
questions.explanation: .gitignore ensures temporary or sensitive files don't clutter the repo.

questions.question_text: What character is used as a wildcard in .gitignore?
questions.explanation: The asterisk matches any number of characters.

questions.question_text: True or False: Git is the same thing as GitHub.
questions.explanation: Git is the local tool; GitHub is a cloud hosting platform for Git repositories.
